content.full_text:
SUPERSEDED
-
-.
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
428 J Street
• Sacramento, CA 958 12-0807
(916) 322-5660
Fax (916) 322-0886
November 9, 1999
Jeffrey G. Johnson
Financial and Political Consulting
305 La Jolla Circle
Salinas, California 93901
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-246
Dear Mr. Johnson:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of council member Charles
Morales regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1 Council member
Morales has authorized you to seek advice on his behalf.
The advice contained in this letter applies to future votes and actions of the council
member only. Please be advised that the Commission does not provide advice concerning past
conduct. (Regulation 1 8329(b)(8)(A).)
QUESTIONS
1. Does council member Morales have an “economic interest” in sources of income to
his estranged wife, including her eniployer?
2. If you find that council member Morales does not have an economic interest in his
spouse’s salary, is the determining factor 12 months living apart or would there be no conflict of
interest due to an “economic interest” as soon as council member Morales was living apart from
his estranged wife and no longer commingling income, in fact paying her voluntary spousal
support?
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-99-246
Page 2
3. Is there any reason why council member Morales should not vote as long as he has
sought two separate legal opinions that both agree that he does not have a conflict of interest?
4. Would council member Morales’ vote on an issue like this likely result in punitive
action by the Fair Political Practices Commission after council member Morales has sought legal
advice and determined himself, based on the facts and the legal advice that a conflict of interest
does not exist?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Under the Act, a public official is generally considered to have an economic interest in
any significant source of income to his or her spouse. In this situation, the council member is
living separate from his spouse, but they are not divorced and there is no courtorder of
separation. In administering the conflict of interest rules of the Act, the Commission necessarily
relies on objective standards rather than the fact-based inquiries of family law. In this case,
where council member Morales has not obtained a divorce or a court order of separation, he is
still considered to have an economic interest in any source of income to his spouse under the Act,
including her employer. If, as you have concluded, the source of income to council member
Morales’ spouse gives rise to a couflict of interest for him in an upcoming city council vote on a
project, then council member Morales is disqualified and must refrain from voting. (You have
not provided us with the facts to independently analyze whether council member Morales has a
conflict under the Act in an upcoming council vote on a project.)
2. As discussed above, we conclude that for purposes ofthe Act, council member
Morales continues to have an economic interest in sources of income to his spouse, including her
employer.
3. Following this advice, council member Morales should refrain from participating in
any vote in which he has a conflict of interest based on his spouse’s salary.
4. Reliance on formal written advice from the Fair Political Practices Commission
confers immunity from prosecution by the Commission on the requestor, and is evidence of good
faith in any other civil or criminal proceeding. As discussed below, the council member’s
reliance on advice from his city attorney does not confer statutory immunity under the Act, and
its bearing on any potential enforcement action would be determined on a case-by-case basis.


File No. A-99-246
Page 3
2
Charles S. Morales is a city council member for the City of Gilroy. His spouse, Ms. Rita
Morales, has worked for 27 years for a company whose sole owner is also an investor in a
project subject to council member Morales’ vote. You question whether council member
Morales has a conflict of interest in an upcoming vote on the project based on his wife’s income.
Council member Morales has been living separately from his estranged wife, Rita Morales, for
over twelve months. She signed a lease on an apartment, and has lived there for over twelve
months. Council member Morales and his wife have not commingled their funds for over twelve
months. Council member Morales has also been paying his wife voluntary spousal support for
over twelve months, though he has not been ordered to do so by a court. You do not know
whether the Morales’ continue to file joint tax returns.
You state that prior to this request for advice, council member Morales sought legal
advice on this question from the City of Gilroy’s attorney. She initially advised him that he had
no conflict of interest in her opinion, but upon further review she recused herself from the matter.
She contacted another attorney to advise council member Morales on this matter, who issued a
nine-page opinion to council member Morales, which you state concluded that Ms. Morales’
income was not an economic interest to council member Morales because they have been living
separately for over a year, have not commingled their salaries, and because Mr. Morales has been
paying voluntary spousal support. You later spoke with staff in the FPPC’s enforcement division
about this issue, who suggested that you request advice from the legal division.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A “public official” is defined in section 82048 and
regulation 18701 as every natural person who is a member, officer, employee, or consultant of a
state or local government agency. This definition includes a member of the Gilroy city council.
1. Economic Interests.
An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official or a member ofhis or her immediate family
or on, among other things:
2 The facts in this request for advice were supplemented by a phone conversation on September 30, 1999.


File No. A-99-246
Page 4
“(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.”
(Section 87103(c).)
Under the Act, the income of a public official includes any community property interest
in the income of a spouse. The Act defines income in section 82030:
“(a) ‘Income’ means, except as provided in subdivision (b), a
payment received, including but not limited to any salary, wage,
advance, dividend, interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, gift,
including any gift of food or beverage, loan, forgiveness or
payment of indebtedness received by the filer, reimbursement for
expenses, per diem, or contribution to an insurance or pension
program paid by any person other than an employer, and including
any communityproperty interest in the income ofa spouse.”
(Emphasis added.)
Thus under the Act, council member Morales would generally be considered to have an
economic interest in any significant source of income to his spouse, Rita Morales, and such a
source of income could give rise to a conflict of interest for him. If any person or business has
been a source of income to council member Morales of $250 or more, or to his spouse of $500 or
more (making his community property interest $250) within the past 12 months, the source of
income is a potentially disqualifying economic interest.
You question whether the income
3 of council member Morales’ spouse from her
employment with a company can still give rise to a conflict of interest for him ifhe and his
spouse are living separately.
The Commission has advised that an official does not have a community property interest
in the income of his or her spouse, where the official and the spouse had a separate property
agreement or a prenuptial agreement providing to that effect.
This case involves only the employment income ofMs. Rita Morales, who is a source of income to
council member Morales. It does not involve an investment interest. An investment interest held by the spouse of
an official, even as separate property, is considered an indirect investment interest of the official which must be
disclosed and may give rise to a conflict of interest. (Section 87103(a); Ryan Advice Letter, No. A-99-027; Johns
Advice Letter, No. A-92-657.)


File No. A-99-246
Page 5
The Vassey Advice Letter, No. A-86-201, advised that a member of the State Water
Resources Control Board did not have a conflict of interest based on income from clients ofher
spouse’s lobbying firm, because (1) the official and her spouse had a formal separate property
agreement with which they had strictly complied and as a result had no community property; and
(2) the spouse’s contracts with clients specified that he would not lobby the State Water
Resources Control Board or the nine regional water boards.
The Katz Advice Letter, No. A-86-335, discussed a proposed prenuptial agreement that
an assembly member and his spouse were considering entering into. The letter advised that if the
prenuptial agreement would convert community property income into separate property income
of his spouse, the official would not be required to report it on his statement of economic
interests.
The Shaw Advice Letter, No. 1-90-377, advised that a planning commissioner need not
disqualify himself from decisions because of income from his spouse’s consulting business
which income was her separate property under a prenuptial agreement. The planning
commissioner’s spouse had a business providing consulting services to a redevelopment agency.
However, since the business itselfwas community property of the couple, the planning
commissioner had to disqualify himself from any decision that would have a foreseeable material
financial effect on the business.
But in this case, Mr. Morales and his spouse have not divorced, and they do not have a
separate property agreement or a court-ordered separation agreement.
In California family law, the determination of a separation date results in the loss of an
economic interest in property by one or the other ofthe spouses. (In re Marriage ofPeters, 52
Cal. App. 4
th 1487 (Cal. App. 3 Dist. 1997).) Family Code section 760 provides that all property
acquired during marriage is community property. However, Family Code section 771 states that
property acquired after a separation is classified as the acquiring spouse’s separate property.
Under family law, determining the date when a married couple separates is a detailed,
fact-based inquiry. In the case In re Marriage ofHardin, 38 Cal. App. 4
th 448 (Cal. App. 4 Dist.,
1.995), the Court ofAppeal summed up the state of California law on determining the date of a
married couple’s separation:
“The courts have neither defined the standard to be employed nor
the factors to be considered in determining the date of separation.
Nevertheless, the answers are implicitly contained within the cases.
All factors bearing on either party’s intentions ‘to return or not to
return to the other spouse’ are to be considered. [Citation omitted.]
No particular facts are per se determinative. The ultimate test is


File No. A-99-246
Page 6
the parties’ subjective intent and all evidence relating to it is to be
objectively considered by the court.” (Id. at 45 1-52.)
Hardin quotes In re Marriage of Umphrey in further discussion about determining the
date of separation:
“Our conclusion that there is nothing sacrosanct about a
separation date recited in a settlement agreement recognizes not
only the equitable nature of the proceedings, but the idiosyncrasies
ofhuman relationships. As this case illustrates, it is not
uncommon for parties to a marriage gone sour to live their lives
separate and apart while maintaining some vestiges ofthe marital
relation. Many marriages are ‘on the rocks’ for protracted periods
oftime and it may be many years before the spouses decide to
formally dissolve their legal relationship. In such situations,
separation dates can often be ‘guesstimates’ or approximations
selected at random or without careful consideration.” (Id. at 452,
quoting In re Marriage of Umphrey, 218 Cal. App. 3d 647, at 657,
fn.2.)
For purposes of administering the conflict-of-interest and reporting rules of the Political
Reform Act, it is neither possible nor desirable for the Commission to undertake an extensive
fact-based inquiry into the private life of a public official to determine whether the official is
separated from his or her spouse. In administering the Act; the Commission needs to rely on
clear, objective criteria with respect to whether the income and investments of the spouse of a
public official are attributable to the official or not.
On another community property question, the Commission chose to adopt a bright-line
rule. With respect to wedding gifts, the Commission stated:
“Rather than depend on the theoretical nuances of community
property law to determine ownership ofwedding gifts, we believe
that the interests of disclosure under the Political Reform Act can
best be served by adopting a test similar to that set forth in Avnet.
Regardless of whether or not the gift is received by the bride prior
to the marriage, wedding gifts benefit both spouses and should be
subject to disclosure unless peculiarly adaptable to the personal use
of one spouse or specifically intended for the use of one spouse.”
(In re Torres, 2 FPPC Op. 31(1975).)
Similarly here, the Commission will not delve into the complexities of family law in
determining whether a married couple is separated, but will rather rely on objective criteria. In


•
File No. A-99-246
Page 7
this case council member Morales has been living separately from Ms. Rita Morales for over 12
months and he has voluntarily been paying her spousal support. However, council member
Morales has not yet gotten divorced or obtained a separation order from a court.
4 Absent a court
order dissolving the legal relationship, the income of the council member Morales’ spouse is
attributable to him and the source ofthat income can give rise to a conflict of interest for him.
2. Reliance on Advice.
You ask whether council member Morales’ vote on an issue like this would likely result
in any punitive action by the Fair Political Practices Commission after council member Morales
has sought legal advice from his city attorney and outside counsel, and determined himselfbased
on the facts and the legal advice from his city attorney and outside counsel that a conflict does
not exist.
V
Reliance on formal written advice from the Commission confers immunity from
prosecution by the Commission on the requestor and is evidence of good faith in any other civil
or criminal proceeding. (Section 83114(b); regulation 18329.) Section 83114(b) provides as
follows:
“(b) Any person may request the commission to provide written
advice with respect to the person’s duties under this title. Such
advice shall be provided within 21 working days of the request,
provided that the time may be extended for good cause. It shall be
a complete defense in any enforcement proceeding initiated by the
commission, and evidence of good faith conduct in any other civil
or criminal proceeding, if the requester, at least 21 working days
prior to the alleged violation, requested written advice from the
commission in good faith, disclosed truthfully all the material
facts, and committed the acts complained of either in reliance on
the advice or because of the failure ofthe commission to provide
advice within 21 days ofthe request or such later extended time.”
Reliance on advice from the council member’s city attorney or outside counsel does not
confer statutory immunity on him. Whether or not the council member’s reliance on the advice
of his city attorney and outside counsel would have bearing in a later Commission enforcement
action if one were initiated would only be determined on a case-by-case basis. In that event, the
Commission would examine whether he reasonably relied on the advice of the city attorney or
If the council member does obtain a divorce or a separation order, his spouse’s employer would continue
to be a disqualifying source of income for 12 months under section 87103(c).


File No. A-99-246
Page 8
outside counsel given the circumstance of the situation and the advice. (West Advice Letter,
No. 1-90-592.)
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Acting Chief, Legal Division
By:
Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:HPW:tls

QUESTION: 1. Does council member Morales have an “economic interest” in sources of income to
his estranged wife, including her eniployer?
2. If you find that council member Morales does not have an economic interest in his
spouse’s salary, is the determining factor 12 months living apart or would there be no conflict of
interest due to an “economic interest” as soon as council member Morales was living apart from
his estranged wife and no longer commingling income, in fact paying her voluntary spousal
support?Page 2
3. Is there any reason why council member Morales should not vote as long as he has
sought two separate legal opinions that both agree that he does not have a conflict of interest?
4. Would council member Morales’ vote on an issue like this likely result in punitive
action by the Fair Political Practices Commission after council member Morales has sought legal
advice and determined himself, based on the facts and the legal advice that a conflict of interest
does not exist?

CONCLUSION: 1. Under the Act, a public official is generally considered to have an economic interest in
any significant source of income to his or her spouse. In this situation, the council member is
living separate from his spouse, but they are not divorced and there is no courtorder of
separation. In administering the conflict of interest rules of the Act, the Commission necessarily
relies on objective standards rather than the fact-based inquiries of family law. In this case,
where council member Morales has not obtained a divorce or a court order of separation, he is
still considered to have an economic interest in any source of income to his spouse under the Act,
including her employer. If, as you have concluded, the source of income to council member
Morales’ spouse gives rise to a couflict of interest for him in an upcoming city council vote on a
project, then council member Morales is disqualified and must refrain from voting. (You have
not provided us with the facts to independently analyze whether council member Morales has a
conflict under the Act in an upcoming council vote on a project.)
2. As discussed above, we conclude that for purposes ofthe Act, council member
Morales continues to have an economic interest in sources of income to his spouse, including her
employer.
3. Following this advice, council member Morales should refrain from participating in
any vote in which he has a conflict of interest based on his spouse’s salary.
4. Reliance on formal written advice from the Fair Political Practices Commission
confers immunity from prosecution by the Commission on the requestor, and is evidence of good
faith in any other civil or criminal proceeding. As discussed below, the council member’s
reliance on advice from his city attorney does not confer statutory immunity under the Act, and
its bearing on any potential enforcement action would be determined on a case-by-case basis.
2
Charles S. Morales is a city council member for the City of Gilroy. His spouse, Ms. Rita
Morales, has worked for 27 years for a company whose sole owner is also an investor in a
project subject to council member Morales’ vote. You question whether council member
Morales has a conflict of interest in an upcoming vote on the project based on his wife’s income.
Council member Morales has been living separately from his estranged wife, Rita Morales, for
over twelve months. She signed a lease on an apartment, and has lived there for over twelve
months. Council member Morales and his wife have not commingled their funds for over twelve
months. Council member Morales has also been paying his wife voluntary spousal support for
over twelve months, though he has not been ordered to do so by a court. You do not know
whether the Morales’ continue to file joint tax returns.
You state that prior to this request for advice, council member Morales sought legal
advice on this question from the City of Gilroy’s attorney. She initially advised him that he had
no conflict of interest in her opinion, but upon further review she recused herself from the matter.
She contacted another attorney to advise council member Morales on this matter, who issued a
nine-page opinion to council member Morales, which you state concluded that Ms. Morales’
income was not an economic interest to council member Morales because they have been living
separately for over a year, have not commingled their salaries, and because Mr. Morales has been
paying voluntary spousal support. You later spoke with staff in the FPPC’s enforcement division
about this issue, who suggested that you request advice from the legal division.
embedding.first_500_words: SUPERSEDED - -. FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION P.O. Box 807 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 958 12-0807 (916) 322-5660 Fax (916) 322-0886 November 9, 1999 Jeffrey G. Johnson Financial and Political Consulting 305 La Jolla Circle Salinas, California 93901 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-99-246 Dear Mr. Johnson: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of council member Charles Morales regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 Council member Morales has authorized you to seek advice on his behalf. The advice contained in this letter applies to future votes and actions of the council member only. Please be advised that the Commission does not provide advice concerning past conduct. (Regulation 1 8329(b)(8)(A).) QUESTIONS 1. Does council member Morales have an “economic interest” in sources of income to his estranged wife, including her eniployer? 2. If you find that council member Morales does not have an economic interest in his spouse’s salary, is the determining factor 12 months living apart or would there be no conflict of interest due to an “economic interest” as soon as council member Morales was living apart from his estranged wife and no longer commingling income, in fact paying her voluntary spousal support? Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-99-246 Page 2 3. Is there any reason why council member Morales should not vote as long as he has sought two separate legal opinions that both agree that he does not have a conflict of interest? 4. Would council member Morales’ vote on an issue like this likely result in punitive action by the Fair Political Practices Commission after council member Morales has sought legal advice and determined himself, based on the facts and the legal advice that a conflict of interest does not exist? CONCLUSIONS 1. Under the Act, a public official is generally considered to have an economic interest in any significant source of income to his or her spouse. In this situation, the council member is living separate from his spouse, but they are not divorced and there is no courtorder of separation. In administering the conflict of interest rules of the Act, the Commission necessarily relies on objective standards rather than the fact-based inquiries of family law. In this case, where council member Morales has not obtained a divorce or a court order of separation, he is still considered to have an economic interest in any source of income to his spouse under the Act, including her employer. If, as you have concluded, the source of income to council member Morales’ spouse gives rise to a couflict of interest for him in an upcoming city council vote on a project, then council member Morales is disqualified and must refrain from voting. (You have not provided us with the facts to independently analyze whether council member Morales has a conflict under the
